shapes.name: tax_line
shapes.subtitle: description
shapes.leading_comment:
A single tax, fee, or surcharge line item on a priced commerce
transaction — one row of the itemized tax/fee breakdown. Any booking
/ order / invoice with N taxes has N tax_line nodes.

Applies broadly: airline ticket taxes, hotel occupancy taxes, rental
car airport fees, restaurant service charges, sales tax, VAT/GST, any
line item that the merchant itemizes separately from the base price.

Why one-node-per-line (rather than `taxes: json[]` on the parent):
1. A single tax can recur per segment / per night / per item. Each
occurrence has its own amount and binding — they're not
aggregable without losing information. (Airline: US
Transportation Tax fires once per leg. Hotel: occupancy tax
fires once per night.)
2. Agent UIs want to show the full breakdown, not a lump-sum
"taxes and fees: $61". Nodes give display, query, and
provenance.
3. Taxes are imposed by third parties (governments, facilities),
not the selling merchant. `imposedBy` makes the authority chain
first-class.

Common tax/fee codes by domain:
airline: US, ZP, XF, AY, YQ, YR (IATA ILTATF list)
hotel: occupancy tax, tourism levy, resort fee
rental car: concession recovery fee, airport access fee
rideshare: booking fee, airport surcharge
commerce: sales tax (state), VAT, GST

Kind (tax | fee | surcharge):
- tax       — government-imposed (US, ZP, AY, VAT, GST, sales tax)
- fee       — facility / service charge (XF, resort fee, concession)
- surcharge — merchant-imposed (YQ fuel surcharge, resort fee)
Matters for refundability: taxes on unflown tickets are often
refundable by law; merchant surcharges often are not.

prior_art.source: IATA List of Ticket and Airport Taxes and Fees (ILTATF)
prior_art.notes: Canonical 1500+ entry registry of 2-char airline tax codes, grouped AT (airport), PC (passenger charge), ST (stamp), TT (ticket), MT (misc). Our `code` = ILTATF code when applicable; `nature` corresponds to ILTATF's grouping.

NDC's Price structure carries Taxes/Tax with TaxCode, CollectionPoint, CountryCode, Nature, Amount, Description. Our code/country/nature/amount/description map 1:1. NDC's Nature enum (security, fuel, facility, tax) informs our values.
Generic commerce. priceComponentType ("Tax"), valueAddedTaxIncluded (our `inclusive`), priceCurrency. Lightweight; we add code, country, `imposedBy` to cover the authority-chain gap.

prior_art.source: UBL 2.1 / Peppol BIS Billing 3.0 — TaxSubtotal / TaxCategory
prior_art.notes: European eInvoicing. TaxSubtotal carries TaxableAmount, TaxAmount, Percent, TaxCategory/TaxScheme (VAT/GST). Our taxableAmount/amount/rate/nature align directly.

Stripe's line-item tax_amounts[] carries amount, inclusive, tax_rate, taxability_reason, taxable_amount. Our inclusive/ rate/taxableAmount match; jurisdiction/jurisdiction_level inspired the country + `imposedBy` split.

Minimal commerce model: price, rate, title, channel_liable. Our amount/rate/description map 1:1. Shopify allows multiple tax_lines per line item with same title + different rates — same pattern we need (US Transportation Tax recurring per segment). We disambiguate via appliesToIndex.

Commerce tax engines. Jurisdiction hierarchy (country / state / county / city / special) and combined tax rate. Informs the `imposedBy: actor` relation for layered jurisdictions (hotel occupancy + tourism + state sales tax, each their own line).

shapes.name: theme
shapes.subtitle: family
shapes.leading_comment:
An OS theme — a named knob-vector over its family's structure.
A theme is data, not code: it picks a value for each of its family's
knobs (`style`, `startMenu`) and bundles a set of assets (fonts,
sounds, wallpaper, icons). It ships NO css and NO structure — the
family (commons/themes/<family>/family.ts) owns those.

Themes are reference data: each `<family>/<theme>/theme.ts` on disk is
extracted into `commons/themes/themes.manifest.json`, and the theme
seeder (core/crates/core/src/sources/themes.rs) upserts one graph node
per theme. The user's `pref:ui.themeId` selects the active theme; the
theme's `style` / `startMenu` are the default knob values, overridable
per-knob via `pref:ui.style` / `pref:ui.startMenu`.

Identity: themeId — unique per source namespace ("windows-xp",
"windows-98"). Every field below is what the seeder writes to the
node; the Rust `Theme` struct is GENERATED from this list
(docs/generate.py), so the seeder cannot write an undeclared val. The
theme's `ontology` block (its product + bundled assets) and its knob
var-groups are NOT node fields — the seeder turns `ontology` into
separate nodes + links, and knob var-groups are frontend-only code.

prior_art.source: System theme APIs (macOS NSAppearance, Windows WinUI)
prior_art.notes: OS-level theme abstraction. Our `family` parallels NSAppearance.Name (aqua, darkAqua) and Windows theme families.

shapes.name: transition
shapes.subtitle: startDate
shapes.leading_comment:
An identity change — name, gender, religion, sports team, anything
identity-shaped — recorded as a point-in-time event. The event captures
the post-change state using the same canonical field names as `birth`;
unfilled fields = "this transition didn't touch that field."

Per platform/README.md rule 1, the date the change took effect rides
on the event (`startDate`). The verb is `changed`:

joe —changed→ transition(givenName: "Joey", startDate: 2010-06-01)

Resolver projection: `person.givenName` walks both `born_in → birth`
and `changed → transition`, picks the latest by `startDate`, returns
the projected field. See `person.yaml` `derived:` block.

Same field set as `birth` (single source of vocabulary for name +
gender + nickname). Future identity axes (religion, race, pronoun,
sports-team affiliation) land here as new optional fields without
touching the resolver — `derived:` bindings on `person.yaml` add
matching `latest:` arms as new dimensions are introduced.

Not jurisdiction-flavoured. A transition may be a legal act (deed
poll, GRC, passport amendment), an administrative filing (Singapore
ICA Change of Particulars), or a non-legal self-identification (going
by a nickname, social-only gender change). The act's legal character,
if any, rides on links from this event to authority/document nodes —
out of scope V1.

prior_art.notes: Past-tense events as facts; entity state = fold over the event stream. `transition` is the past-tense event-node; the `latest:` resolver is the fold. Reuses canonical field names — no `new_*` prefix per the event-sourcing convention.

Palantir reifies every mutation as a queryable Action Log Object — same shape as our reified `transition` event. They type Actions per-mutation (`AssignEmployee` etc.); we collapse to one umbrella shape with optional fields for agent-OS ergonomics (operations not known in advance).

prior_art.source: IMO / GISIS maritime registry — particulars change
prior_art.notes: 50-year-old domain proves the pattern: stable identifier (IMO number) + canonical property names + reified change events (name/flag/owner change). `Person.id` ↔ `Ship.imo`; `transition.gender` ↔ a `flag_change` on a ship.

FHIR encodes dated names via multi-value on the Patient (no event-node). We lift the same pattern to an event-node because the change has its own date + place + authority context worth capturing as a first-class graph entity.
Property statements with start-time/end-time qualifiers. Validates "reuse canonical field name + date positions in time".

    \ same shape as our reified `transition` event. They type Actions per-mutation\
    \ (`AssignEmployee` etc.); we collapse to one umbrella shape with optional fields\
    \ for agent-OS ergonomics (operations not known in advance)."
- source: "IMO / GISIS maritime registry \u2014 particulars change"
  url: https://gisis.imo.org/public/default.aspx
  notes: "50-year-old domain proves the pattern: stable identifier (IMO number) +\
    \ canonical property names + reified change events (name/flag/owner change). `Person.id`\
    \ \u2194 `Ship.imo`; `transition.gender` \u2194 a `flag_change` on a ship."
- source: FHIR R5 HumanName.use + HumanName.period
  url: https://hl7.org/fhir/datatypes.html#HumanName

    We lift the same pattern to an event-node because the change has its own date
    + place + authority context worth capturing as a first-class graph entity.
- source: Wikidata P735/P734/P21 + P580/P582 qualifiers
  url: https://www.wikidata.org/wiki/Property:P735

    canonical field name + date positions in time".

shapes.name: trip
shapes.subtitle: tripType
shapes.leading_comment:
A directed journey from origin to destination — one direction of travel.
A round-trip booking produces two trips (outbound + return).
A single Uber ride is one trip. A connecting flight is one trip with multiple legs.
Multi-stop Uber rides have multiple legs (one per waypoint pair).

Terminology alignment:
Duffel/NDC "slice" = trip    |  "segment" = leg
SerpAPI result = trip        |  flights[] element = leg
Schema.org Trip > subTrip    |  Uber ride = trip (1+ legs)
Train journey = trip         |  each train boarded = leg

prior_art.notes: Our origin/destination/departureTime/arrivalTime map exactly; legs[] ≈ subTrip or itinerary.
NDC slice = our trip; NDC segment = our leg. cabinClass, bookingToken come from NDC offer items.
Practical source for ride trips. Our fare/fareAmount/ trackingUrl/isSurge/isScheduled lifted from Uber's Trip model.

shapes.name: unit
shapes.subtitle: symbol
shapes.leading_comment:
A unit of measure — a concrete scale for a quantity. mg/dL, USD, pascal,
day. Identified primarily by its UCUM code.

A unit carries the data for INTRINSIC conversion: a constant factor and
an affine offset to the coherent base unit of its dimension
(value_in_base = value * toBaseFactor + toBaseOffset). Conversions that
need EXTERNAL context — a currency FX rate, a substance's molar mass —
are NOT here; they are `conversion` nodes. The dividing line: if the
number depends on anything other than the two units, it is not a unit
property.

There is no single authoritative unit-code registry — no "ISO 4217 for
units". Units predate computing by centuries and the space is
fragmented: ISO 80000 defines units in prose and assigns no codes; NIST
publishes guidance only. So the unit node carries SEVERAL cross-
reference identifiers, the way an airline carries both an IATA and an
ICAO code:
ucumCode              — the universal working identity (UCUM)
siDigitalFrameworkUri — the authoritative SI reference (BIPM), when
the unit is part of the SI; NULL otherwise,
and that null is itself a machine-checkable
assertion "this unit is not SI"
unCefactCommonCode    — the trade/interop code (== schema.org unitCode)
qudtUnitIri           — the semantic-web cross-reference hub
wikidataId            — the universal glue identifier

`kind` distinguishes unit families that behave differently under
conversion — the engine must branch on it:
physical    — real SI dimension, intrinsic linear/affine conversion
currency    — ISO 4217, no real dimension; conversion is contextual (FX)
logarithmic — pH, dB, Richter — conversion is exponential, not linear
arbitrary   — UCUM arbitrary units ([IU], [CFU]) — no conversion at all
count       — dimensionless tallies

`name` (inherited) is the human label, e.g. "milligram per deciliter".

prior_art.source: UCUM — Unified Code for Units of Measure
prior_art.notes: ucumCode is the primary working identity — case-sensitive variant (mg is not MG; h hour is not H henry). Compositional, and the de-facto healthcare standard (FHIR Quantity mandates it, LOINC and HL7 use it). Machine-readable database: ucum-essence.xml.

prior_art.source: BIPM — SI Digital Framework / SI Reference Point
prior_art.notes: Launched March 2024 by the BIPM CIPM Task Group on the Digital SI. Publishes resolvable persistent URIs for SI units, prefixes, and defining constants (base namespace si-digital-framework.org/SI/ units/), served as TTL plus a JSON/XML API. The most authoritative source for SI units — treaty-level. siDigitalFrameworkUri carries it; null for non-SI units, and that null is meaningful.

prior_art.source: UN/CEFACT Recommendation 20 — Codes for Units of Measure Used in International Trade
prior_art.notes: Around 700 short codes (KGM kilogram, MTR metre, LTR litre, CEL degree Celsius). Rev 17 (2021), freely downloadable as XLSX or genericode XML. schema.org's unitCode property uses exactly these codes — unCefactCommonCode is the interop join key.

prior_art.source: QUDT — Quantities, Units, Dimensions and Types
prior_art.notes: qudtUnitIri cross-references QUDT, itself a cross-reference hub (QUDT units carry qudt:ucumCode, qudt:uneceCommonCode, qudt:wikidataMatch). toBaseFactor/toBaseOffset correspond to qudt:conversionMultiplier / conversionOffset; kind=currency corresponds to qudt:CurrencyUnit, which also carries NO conversion multiplier — FX is external.
wikidataId (kilogram = Q11570) is a universal glue identifier — links to every Wikipedia language edition and many external databases. Free SPARQL endpoint plus dumps.

iso4217 (alpha) plus iso4217Numeric and minorExponent for currency units. ISO 4217 IS the authoritative currency-code registry — notably, currency is the ONE unit family with a single authoritative code system; physical units have none.
Precedent that the minimum unit model is (value, unitCode), and that unitCode in practice means a UN/CEFACT Rec 20 code.

prior_art.source: NIST SP 811 / SP 330 — and why NIST is NOT a field
prior_art.notes: Recorded deliberately. NIST publishes SI usage guidance (SP 811) and the US edition of the SI Brochure (SP 330) — prose only. NIST assigns no unit codes and maintains no unit registry; there is nothing to cross-reference, so no NIST field exists on this shape. Same for ISO 80000 — it defines units in prose and assigns no codes.

shapes.name: user
shapes.subtitle: name
shapes.leading_comment:
An AgentOS user — the seat at this machine. Carries the user's
preference state: pref:ui (render) and pref:system (engine behavior),
resolved through the V6/V8 cascade alongside shape-def + instance
overrides. Linked to a person via --identified_as--> when the user
is human-identified; linkless users are anonymous.

Distinct from `person` (universal human shape — schema.org / vCard)
and `agent` (AI actor). person stays clean of AgentOS-specific
fields; user is the carrier for everything OS-runtime.

`prefsSchemas` (top-level metadata, not a per-instance field) declares
the universal pref vocabulary for AgentOS users. The engine seeds
these onto the USER shape-def node at boot; SettingsPanel reads them
off the def, walks the entries, and writes resolved values back to
the active user instance under `pref:<namespace>`. The cascade does
the rest.

prior_art.notes: The OS-level "user" — uid + login name + home dir. Our `osUsername` mirrors `pw_name`; identity-by-OS-account follows the same pattern. We diverge by separating the OS seat (`user`) from the human (`person`); POSIX conflates them.
Loose fit. schema.org has no "OS user" concept — user accounts are product-specific. We model it explicitly because AgentOS is the product, and the user-as-pref-carrier is load-bearing.

The Apple model: per-user preference store, keyed by the OS account. Our `pref:ui` / `pref:system` blobs play the same role, but live in the graph (queryable, multi-process safe) rather than a plist file.

shapes.name: user_identity
shapes.subtitle: volume_id
shapes.leading_comment:
An identity claim — "the engine-level user X identifies as person:Y
in volume Z". Phase 5 of the volumes spec; lets one OS user map onto
multiple persona-nodes across multiple mounted volumes.

`user_id` is foreign to `engine.db::users` — the engine's user
table — not to a graph node. A user_identity row is the bridge.

Many mappings per user are normal:
user joe → person:joe in users/joe.db                 (active=true)
user joe → person:harry_potter in pods/hp.db          (active=false; role-play)
user joe → person:joe_health_subject in pods/health.db (active=true; explicit subject)

The `active` flag picks the "default" person inside each volume when
the user has multiple mappings into the same one.

prior_art.notes: Solid Pod ↔ WebID is the per-user identity model. A Solid user owns one personal pod whose WebID is the authoritative identity; cross-pod identity is a single relation. We split: User is engine state; Person is graph content; user_identity is the explicit bridge that can be many-to-many across volumes.
OS users (engine.db::users) and home directories (~/.agentos/users/<u>.db) follow the Unix convention. The identity *within* the home is content the home owns.

shapes.raw_body:
plural: user_identities
display:
  subtitle: volume_id
  highlights:
  - person_node_id
  - active
icon: user
fields:
  user_id: string
  volume_id: string
  person_node_id: string
  active: boolean
prior_art:
- source: Solid (Tim Berners-Lee)
  url: https://solidproject.org/faq
  notes: "Solid Pod \u2194 WebID is the per-user identity model. A Solid user owns\
    \ one personal pod whose WebID is the authoritative identity; cross-pod identity\
    \ is a single relation. We split: User is engine state; Person is graph content;\
    \ user_identity is the explicit bridge that can be many-to-many across volumes."
- source: "Unix/macOS \u2014 /etc/passwd vs /Users/<u>"
  url: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Passwd
  notes: OS users (engine.db::users) and home directories (~/.agentos/users/<u>.db)
    follow the Unix convention. The identity *within* the home is content the home
    owns.

shapes.name: video
shapes.subtitle: author
shapes.leading_comment:
A video file — the media artifact, not the social context around it.
Engagement metrics (views, likes, comments) belong on the post that
contains this video, not on the video itself. A video on your hard
drive has no likes.

A video is BOTH a creative_work (the film / clip is the work — its
director, producer, license, datePublished come from there) AND a
file (the bytes on disk, with a specific codec / resolution).

prior_art.notes: Our durationMs ≈ duration (ISO 8601 period); resolution ≈ videoFrameSize; frameRate has no direct property; codec ≈ encodingFormat.
Our codec values map to registered video/* media types (mp4, webm, ogg).
Canonical codec definitions. Our codec values are MPEG/ITU codec short names (h264, vp9, av1).

shapes.name: volume
shapes.subtitle: kind
shapes.leading_comment:
A mounted Volume — any named source of typed nodes the engine has
attached to the Registry: a `.db` memex file, a YAML text volume, or a
transport volume served live by an app (the host filesystem, a USB
stick, a browser's data). The home graph is the always-mounted
writeable Volume; it is the hardcoded constant. Every OTHER mount IS a
`volume` node — one row per mount, persisted so the mount survives
engine restart (`auto_mount: true`).

Mount lifecycle is six steps; this shape captures the persisted state
of step 5. See `core/_roadmap/p2/realms-transports/plan.md`.

prior_art.notes: RDF binary file format; one file = one queryable graph. The Header describes the dataset in its own vocabulary — same shapes-ride-along move as our embedded ontology.
Register an external source under a URI; query as a named graph. Our volume shape is the registration row; mount/unmount is the verb pair.
The UX mental model. A volume is an attached, browseable disk; the Finder shows it in the sidebar; eject detaches it without destroying the underlying file.

auth_contracts.description: Return shape for `@provides(cookie_auth, ...)` app operations. Top-level dict with `cookies: [Cookie, ...]`; each Cookie has a `name` + `value` and optional `expires` / `created` for freshness arbitration in crates/auth/src/source.rs.

required.name: domain
required.description: The cookie domain this batch is scoped to (e.g. "app.greptile.com"). The engine uses this to bind the cookies to the right host.

required.name: cookies
required.description: Array of Cookie dicts (see `cookie_fields` below for the per- element shape). Empty array is valid (means "no cookies for this domain") and causes the resolver to skip this provider.

recommended.name: count
recommended.description: Length of `cookies` — purely informational, the engine recomputes from `cookies.len()`. Useful for app-side assertions and trace summaries.

recommended.name: source
recommended.description: Provider identifier for trace + diagnostic output (e.g. "brave-browser", "firefox", "playwright"). Helps the user tell which browser the cookies came from.

required.name: name
required.description: Cookie name. Used by the engine to construct the `name=value` pairs for the Cookie request header (crates/auth/src/source.rs::score_provider).

required.name: value
required.description: Cookie value. Joined with name as `name=value`; multiple cookies joined by `; `. Engine does not URL-decode.

recommended.name: expires
recommended.description: Unix timestamp (seconds, fractional ok) when the cookie expires. The engine filters expired cookies in `score_provider`; cookies without an `expires` are treated as session cookies and kept. `expiry` is accepted as an alias for compatibility.

recommended.name: created
recommended.description: Unix timestamp (seconds, fractional ok) when the cookie was set. Used for freshness arbitration when multiple providers return overlapping cookies — the newer one wins. Browsers like Brave/Firefox supply this; Playwright/CDP doesn't.

recommended.name: domain
recommended.description: Cookie's own domain (may differ from envelope domain on subdomains).

auth_contracts.description: Return shape for `@provides(oauth_auth, ...)` app operations. Engine consumes via crates/auth/src/types.rs (Session::to_auth_header, auth_fields) and crates/auth/src/resolve.rs (token-refresh path).

required.name: access_token
required.description: OAuth 2.0 access token. Used by the engine to construct the Authorization: Bearer <token> header for downstream API calls. Required — missing this is the single bug that motivated this contract.

recommended.name: refresh_token
recommended.description: OAuth 2.0 refresh token. The engine refreshes automatically when the access_token is near expiry; without this the token stops working at expires_in seconds and the user must re-auth.

recommended.name: expires_in
recommended.description: Seconds until access_token expiry, per RFC 6749 §5.1. Engine derives `expires_at = now + expires_in` at store time. If omitted the engine assumes 1 hour (3600s).

recommended.name: scope
recommended.description: Space-separated list of granted scopes. Surfaced to the user via `agentos call accounts` for visibility into what the credential can do. Engine parses this into a structured `scopes` array (crates/auth/src/resolve.rs::inject_scopes_array).

recommended.name: client_id
recommended.description: OAuth client ID — required for the engine to perform token refresh on its own (without re-invoking the provider). If omitted, refresh requires re-running the provider app.

recommended.name: token_url
recommended.description: OAuth token endpoint URL — same as client_id, required for engine-driven token refresh. If omitted, refresh round-trips through the provider app.

ops.name: auth_store.read
ops.doc: Read the best-scored decrypted credential row for a (domain, item_type, account?) tuple.
ops.doc_full:
Read one row from the encrypted credentials store. Returns the
decrypted JSON `value` plus identifier / source / last_verified.
Domain-normalization happens on the caller side — pass the
canonical credential-store form (e.g. `".approach.app"`).

Args:
    domain: Canonical credential-store domain.
    item_type: Credential kind (`"login_credentials"`, `"cookie"`, ...).
    account: Optional identifier disambiguator.

Returns:
    `{found, identifier?, value?, source?, last_verified?}`. On
    no-match, `found` is `false` and every other field is absent.

request.name: domain
request.doc: Canonical credential-store domain.

request.name: item_type
request.doc: Credential kind (login_credentials, cookie, oauth, api_key, …).

request.name: account
request.doc: Optional identifier disambiguator.

fields.name: value
fields.doc: Decrypted JSON payload; shape depends on item_type.

fields.name: source
fields.doc: Where the row came from (onepassword, brave-browser, …).

fields.name: last_verified
fields.doc: Unix timestamp of the most recent successful verification.

ops.leading_comment:
auth_store.* ops — encrypted credential store reads.

The only SDK surface onto the encrypted credentials table. The
encryption key lives in the macOS Keychain; Python never holds it.

ops.name: blobs.put
ops.doc: Store base64 bytes content-addressed under ~/.agentos/blobs; returns path, sha256, size.
ops.doc_full:
Store bytes in the engine's content-addressed blob store:
`~/.agentos/blobs/<sha256[0:2]>/<sha256>.<ext>`. Identical bytes land
on the same path, so re-storing is a free no-op — dedup by
construction. The returned absolute path is what a file-shaped graph
node carries in its `path` field.

Args:
    data: base64-encoded bytes.
    ext: filename extension (no dot); names the stored file.

Returns:
    path, sha256, size of the stored blob.

request.name: data
request.doc: Base64-encoded bytes to store.

request.name: ext
request.doc: File extension (no dot), e.g. jpg, ogg, pdf.

fields.name: path
fields.doc: Absolute path of the stored blob.

fields.name: size
fields.doc: Byte length of the decoded payload.

ops.leading_comment:
blobs.* ops — the content-addressed blob store at ~/.agentos/blobs.

Engine-owned bytes on disk; graph nodes reference blobs by the returned
path (a file-shaped node's `path` field). Apps hand the engine base64
and the engine owns placement, hashing, and dedup — no app ever
invents a path into the user's state dir. Handlers live in
agentos-exec-executors.

ops.name: blobs.get
ops.doc: Read one blob back from ~/.agentos/blobs by absolute path; returns base64 data, sha256, size.
ops.doc_full:
Read bytes back from the engine's content-addressed blob store —
the read half of `blobs.put`, behind the same policy gate. The
path must resolve inside `~/.agentos/blobs`; the engine refuses
anything else, so an app can only ever read bytes the store
already owns — never an arbitrary file.

Args:
    path: absolute blob-store path (from `blobs.put`, or the
        `path` field of a hydrated attachment).

Returns:
    data (base64), sha256, size of the blob.

request.name: path
request.doc: Absolute path inside ~/.agentos/blobs.

ops.leading_comment:
blobs.* ops — the content-addressed blob store at ~/.agentos/blobs.

Engine-owned bytes on disk; graph nodes reference blobs by the returned
path (a file-shaped node's `path` field). Apps hand the engine base64
and the engine owns placement, hashing, and dedup — no app ever
invents a path into the user's state dir. Handlers live in
agentos-exec-executors.

ops.name: crypto.pbkdf2
ops.doc: PBKDF2-HMAC-SHA1 key derivation. Returns hex-encoded bytes.
ops.doc_full:
PBKDF2-HMAC-SHA1 key derivation. Used by browser apps to reproduce
Chrome's Safe Storage key stretch.

Args:
    password, salt: opaque strings.
    iterations: default 1.
    length: output byte length (default 16).

Returns:
    Derived key as bytes (hex-decoded by the SDK stub).

request.name: password
request.doc: Password to stretch.

request.name: salt
request.doc: Salt (opaque bytes as a string).

request.name: iterations
request.doc: Iteration count.

request.name: length
request.doc: Desired output length in bytes.

ops.leading_comment:
crypto.* ops — key derivation and symmetric decryption.

Used almost exclusively by browser apps decrypting stored cookies.
Handlers live in agentos-exec-executors.

ops.name: crypto.aes
ops.doc: AES-128-CBC decrypt. Key/data/iv are hex. Returns hex plaintext.
ops.doc_full:
AES-128-CBC decrypt. Key, ciphertext, and IV are all hex-encoded.
The returned plaintext is hex-encoded on the wire and decoded to
bytes by the SDK stub.

Args:
    key: hex (16 bytes = 32 hex chars for AES-128).
    data: ciphertext, hex.
    iv: hex. Default: 32 zero chars (Chrome convention).

Returns:
    Plaintext bytes.

request.name: key
request.doc: Key as hex (16 bytes / 32 hex chars for AES-128).

request.name: data
request.doc: Ciphertext as hex.

request.name: iv
request.doc: Initialization vector as hex. Defaults to 32 zero hex chars.

ops.leading_comment:
crypto.* ops — key derivation and symmetric decryption.

Used almost exclusively by browser apps decrypting stored cookies.
Handlers live in agentos-exec-executors.

ops.name: http.request
ops.doc: Stateless HTTP op. SDK callers use client.get/post/… — this op is their shared backend.
ops.doc_full:
Stateless HTTP op. Every verb-dispatched SDK method (`client.get`,
`client.post`, …) routes through this; app code does not call
`http.request` directly. No cookie-jar state survives between calls —
the SDK's ambient Jar serializes cookies into the request's Cookie:
header and feeds Set-Cookie responses back into itself.

Args:
    url, method, headers, params, body, http2, timeout.

Returns:
    HttpResponse {status, body?, body_bytes?, headers, url, body_length, content_encoding}.

request.name: url
request.doc: Absolute URL.

request.name: method
request.doc: HTTP method: GET, POST, PUT, DELETE, PATCH, HEAD.

request.name: headers
request.doc: Request headers. Cookies ride here under "Cookie".

request.name: params
request.doc: Query string parameters; appended to the URL.

request.name: body
request.doc: Raw request body — bytes or string, sent verbatim.

request.name: http2
request.doc: Whether to enable HTTP/2. Set false for JA4-fingerprint hosts.

request.name: timeout
request.doc: Request timeout in seconds.

fields.name: url
fields.doc: Final URL after the redirect chain.

fields.name: content_encoding
fields.doc: Content encoding (gzip, br, zstd, or none).

ops.name: llm.resolve_tools
ops.doc: Resolve app-scoped tool refs ("app.operation") to provider-native tool defs.
ops.doc_full:
Resolve a list of app-scoped tool refs (`"app.operation"`) into
provider-native tool definitions. Used by llm.agent loops that need
to pass the router's tool list into a chat call.

Args:
    tools: list of `"app.operation"` strings.

Returns:
    {tools: [tool_def, …]}.

request.name: tools
request.doc: Tool references (app.operation).

fields.name: tools
fields.doc: Resolved tool definitions, one per input ref (in order).

ops.leading_comment:
llm.* ops — schema resolution.

llm.resolve_tools walks apps' `provides` entries to build provider-format
tool definitions. Handler lives in agentos-core.

ops.name: plist.parse
ops.doc: Parse a hex-encoded NSKeyedArchiver binary plist.
ops.doc_full:
Parse a hex-encoded NSKeyedArchiver binary plist and extract specific
object indices.

Args:
    hex_data: The plist as hex.
    extract: Mapping of {field_name: object_index} in the archived graph.

Returns:
    Dict of {field_name: value}.

request.name: hex_data
request.doc: Hex-encoded plist bytes.

request.name: extract
request.doc: Extraction map: field_name → object_index in the archived graph.

ops.leading_comment:
plist.* ops — NSKeyedArchiver binary plist parsing.

Used by browser apps to parse keychain-stored NSKeyedArchiver blobs.
Handlers live in agentos-exec-executors.

ops.name: secrets.read
ops.doc: Read a UTF-8 secret from the OS-level secret store.
ops.doc_full:
Read a UTF-8 secret from the platform secret store.

Args:
    service: Keychain service name (e.g. "Chrome Safe Storage").
    account: Account name. Defaults to the current $USER.

Returns:
    The secret as a UTF-8 string.

request.name: service
request.doc: Keychain service name.

request.name: account
request.doc: Keychain account name; defaults to the current $USER.

response.doc: Secret value as a UTF-8 string.

ops.leading_comment:
secrets.* ops — OS-level secret store reads.

Platform secret store (macOS keychain / Windows Credential Manager /
Linux Secret Service), chosen at compile time in agentos-secrets.

ops.name: secrets.read_binary
ops.doc: Read a binary secret. Bytes are hex-encoded on the wire.
ops.doc_full:
Read a binary secret from the platform secret store. Used for
NSKeyedArchiver blobs (Chrome Safe Storage binary entries, etc.).

Bytes travel hex-encoded; the generated SDK stub decodes to bytes.

Args:
    service: Keychain service name.
    account: Account name. Defaults to the current $USER.

Returns:
    The secret value as bytes (decoded from hex by the SDK stub).

request.name: service
request.doc: Keychain service name.

request.name: account
request.doc: Keychain account name; defaults to the current $USER.

ops.leading_comment:
secrets.* ops — OS-level secret store reads.

Platform secret store (macOS keychain / Windows Credential Manager /
Linux Secret Service), chosen at compile time in agentos-secrets.

ops.name: services.call
ops.doc: Match an app providing service `name` and dispatch its tool, returning the tool's result unchanged.
ops.doc_full:
Matchmake a provider for service `name` and dispatch `verb` on it.
The tool's return value is passed through unchanged — no envelope.

Used by SDK wrappers (`credentials.retrieve`, `llm.oneshot`, etc.)
and by apps that consume a service across app boundaries without
naming the provider.

Args:
    name: service name (e.g. `"login_credentials"`, `"llm"`).
    verb: tool to invoke on the picked provider. Defaults to the
          provider's declared `via`.
    params: parameters forwarded to the picked tool, unchanged.
    app: optional app-id override; forces a specific provider.

Returns:
    The picked tool's return value, unchanged.

request.name: name
request.doc: Service name to match.

request.name: verb
request.doc: Tool-name override; defaults to the provider's declared via.

request.name: params
request.doc: Parameters for the picked tool. Passed through unchanged.

request.name: app
request.doc: App-id override; forces a specific provider.

response.doc: The picked tool's return value, unchanged — no envelope.

ops.leading_comment:
services.* ops — generic service dispatch.

Apps never name each other. Consumers ask for a service; the engine
walks the apps providing it, picks one (the service node's
`defaults_to` app first, then the usual ranking), dispatches.

ops.name: services.list_providers
ops.doc: List every app providing service `name` with its ranking and credential state.
ops.doc_full:
Enumerate apps that provide service `name`. Returns the ranked
candidate list without dispatching any tool — useful for
introspection, provider selection, or iterating providers with
per-provider fallback logic in app code.

Args:
    name: service name.
    model: optional model filter for `llm` service matches.

Returns:
    `{providers: [{app_id, via, cred_state, urls?}, ...]}`.

request.name: name
request.doc: Service name to enumerate providers for.

request.name: model
request.doc: Optional model filter for llm service matches.

ops.leading_comment:
services.* ops — generic service dispatch.

Apps never name each other. Consumers ask for a service; the engine
walks the apps providing it, picks one (the service node's
`defaults_to` app first, then the usual ranking), dispatches.

ops.name: shell.run
ops.doc: Run a binary with controlled arguments. Shell interpreters are blocked.
ops.doc_full:
Execute a binary with controlled argv. The invocation is NOT shell-parsed,
and shell interpreters (sh, bash, zsh, fish, etc.) are rejected to prevent
command-injection shortcuts.

Args:
    binary, args, timeout, cwd, input.

Returns:
    {exit_code, stdout, stderr}.

request.name: binary
request.doc: Binary to execute (absolute path or $PATH name).

request.name: args
request.doc: Argv tail — not shell-parsed.

request.name: timeout
request.doc: Timeout in seconds.

request.name: cwd
request.doc: Working directory. Defaults to the process CWD.

request.name: input
request.doc: Optional stdin bytes written to the child as UTF-8.

fields.name: exit_code
fields.doc: Exit code; -1 if the child was signalled.

ops.name: sql.query
ops.doc: Run a read-only SQL query against a named SQLite file.
ops.doc_full:
Run a read-only SQL query against a named SQLite file.

Args:
    sql: Parameterized SQL text. Use `:name` placeholders.
    db: Path to the SQLite file (`~` expands to $HOME).
    params: Named bind parameters (optional).
    attach: Alias → path map for ATTACH DATABASE (optional).

Returns:
    List of row dicts keyed by column name.

request.name: sql
request.doc: Parameterized SQL text. Use :name placeholders bound via params.

request.name: db
request.doc: Path to the SQLite file. ~ is expanded to $HOME.

request.name: params
request.doc: Named bind parameters — mapped onto :name placeholders.

request.name: attach
request.doc: Alias → path map for ATTACH DATABASE — joins across files.

response.doc: Rows — each a JSON object keyed by column name.

ops.leading_comment:
sql.* ops — SQLite query/execute against a named database file.

Handlers live in agentos-graph-executors.

ops.name: sql.execute
ops.doc: Run a writing SQL statement against a named SQLite file.
ops.doc_full:
Run a writing SQL statement (INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE/DDL).

Args:
    sql: SQL text to execute.
    db: Path to the SQLite file.

Returns:
    {rows_affected, last_insert_rowid?}.

request.name: sql
request.doc: SQL text to execute.

request.name: db
request.doc: Path to the SQLite file. ~ is expanded to $HOME.

fields.name: last_insert_rowid
fields.doc: Rowid of the last INSERT, if applicable.

ops.leading_comment:
sql.* ops — SQLite query/execute against a named database file.

Handlers live in agentos-graph-executors.

fields.name: app_id
fields.doc: App id that provides the service.

fields.name: via
fields.doc: Tool name (via) on the app that would be invoked.

fields.name: cred_state
fields.doc: Credential state: not_required, present, or missing.

fields.name: urls
fields.doc: Optional URL patterns declared on the provides binding.

services.name: api_key
services.description: Credential provision — an API key for a platform. Same matchmaking path as login_credentials; secret material never transits the LLM.

platform.doc: The platform the key is for.

services.leading_comment: An API key for a platform (credential provision).

services.name: browser_session
services.description: High-level, typed session API for driving a live browser: a provider exposes `session_open / navigate / evaluate / on_event / get_cookies / get_storage / close`, hiding the protocol. This is what reverse-engineering, scraping, and interaction-driving apps consume — never `cdp_access` directly.
services.leading_comment: High-level typed browser session API on top of cdp_access.

services.name: cdp_access
services.description: Raw, protocol-level browser access. A provider returns a CDP WebSocket URL + target descriptor for a debug-attachable Chromium browser. Tiny surface: one `cdp_connect` tool. Brave is today's provider; Chrome/Arc could join. Consumers should almost always use `browser_session` instead — this is the layer underneath it.
services.leading_comment: Raw protocol-level access to a debug-attachable Chromium browser.

services.name: cookie_auth
services.description: Auth provision — return fresh cookies for a domain from a live browser profile. Consumed by the engine's auth resolution, never called ad hoc; freshness arbitration picks the newest cookie per domain across providers.
services.leading_comment: Provide fresh browser cookies for a domain (auth provision).

services.name: email_lookup
services.description: Look up a person's email address from identity signals (name, organization, domain).
services.leading_comment: Find a person's email address from name/domain signals.

services.name: file_info
services.description: Return one file's metadata (size, kind, timestamps) without reading its contents.
services.leading_comment: Stat one file — size, kind, timestamps.

services.name: file_list
services.description: List the entries under a filesystem path, as typed `file` rows.

services.name: file_read
services.description: Read a file's contents by path.

services.name: flight_search
services.description: Search for flights — origin, destination, dates — returning typed flight/fare results.
services.leading_comment: Search flights between airports on a date.

services.name: geocoding
services.description: Resolve a place name or address to coordinates (and back). Returns typed `place` rows.

services.name: llm
services.description: LLM inference. Providers declare which models they serve; the SDK's matchmaking (`agentos.llm`) ranks providers per model. There is no "LLM" category in the engine — it's just another service.
services.leading_comment: LLM inference — completions/chat from a model provider.

services.name: login_credentials
services.description: Credential provision — an `{email|handle, password}` tuple for a domain, from a password manager, keychain, or vault. Wired through the engine's `credentials.retrieve` matchmaking, so login tools resolve their inputs without the LLM ever seeing raw secret values.
services.leading_comment: {email|handle, password} tuple for a domain (credential provision).

services.name: map_tiles
services.description: Serve map tiles / static map imagery for coordinates and zoom level. Declared ahead of a shipping provider so the interface name is fixed.
services.leading_comment: Serve map tile imagery for a viewport.

services.name: oauth_auth
services.description: Auth provision — return an OAuth credential (access token + refresh metadata) for a platform. Consumed by the engine's auth resolution; the token-refresh path re-dispatches the provider when expiry nears.
services.leading_comment: Provide OAuth credentials for a platform (auth provision).

services.name: password
services.description: Credential provision — a password for a caller-supplied identifier. Same matchmaking path as login_credentials; secret material never transits the LLM.

identifier.doc: The account/identifier the password belongs to.

services.leading_comment: A password for a caller-supplied identifier (credential provision).

services.name: signs_for
services.description: Reserved for passkey / hardware-key providers that sign challenges rather than returning secret material. No resolver ships yet; the name is fixed now so the interface shape is stable.

services.name: volume_transport
services.description: The providing app announces mountable volumes and serves their contents to the finder/graph layer. One service, a fixed three-verb interface: `list_volumes()` → volume[] (ANNOUNCE — called at boot and on refresh; entries upsert as volume nodes under a realm), `list_contents(id, cursor?)` → typed child nodes + nextCursor (SERVE), `read_node(id)` → one node's full detail. The provides binding (`via`) names `list_volumes` only — it marks the app as a transport; `list_contents` / `read_node` are reached by their fixed names on the announcing app (browse binds to the stamped provider, never a fresh service walk). Read-only by construction: the write verbs don't exist yet. Contract: core/_roadmap/p2/realms-transports/plan.md "The transport contract".
services.leading_comment: Announce + serve mountable volumes (the KIO-worker / 9P read-only core).

services.name: web_read
services.description: Fetch a URL and return the page as a `webpage` node with a content row — readable text, not raw bytes. Plain HTTP fetch is the baseline technique; JS-rendering providers (CDP-backed) and extraction APIs (Firecrawl, Exa) compete on quality, not interface.
services.leading_comment: Fetch one URL and return it as a webpage with content.

services.name: web_search
services.description: Search the web for a query; results land on the graph as `result` rows. The baseline discovery service — most installed search apps provide it.
services.leading_comment: Search the web. Providers: Brave Search, Exa, SerpAPI, …
